notes: Paralyzed man in Lydda healed through Peter's ministry in Acts 9.
Prophet who comes down from Judea and warns Paul in Acts 21.
Client king who comes to Caesarea and hears Paul's case in Acts 25.
Figure put forward in the Ephesian uproar who attempts to address the crowd in Acts 19.
High priest who comes down to Caesarea to accuse Paul in Acts 24.
Disciple in Damascus who restores Saul's sight and baptizes him in Acts 9.
High priest who convenes the Sanhedrin that condemns James in Josephus Antiquities 20.200.
Brother of Simon Peter.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as Paul's kinsman and fellow prisoner.
Faithful witness named among the believers in Pergamum in Revelation 2.
Alexandrian Christian teacher active at Ephesus and in Achaia.
Believer greeted alongside Philemon and Archippus in the Epistle to Philemon.
Jewish Christian coworker of Paul associated with Corinth and Ephesus.
Believer greeted in Philemon and mentioned in connection with Colossae.
Thessalonian companion of Paul named among the Acts 20 travel party.
Athenian Christian philosopher who wrote an early apology addressed to the emperor Hadrian or Antoninus Pius c. 125-130.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Athenian Christian philosopher who addressed an embassy to Marcus Aurelius and Commodus c. 177 defending Christians and articulating Logos theology.
Early missionary coworker of Paul and prominent figure in Antioch.
Member of the Twelve in the Synoptic apostolic lists.
Royal figure who comes with Agrippa to Caesarea and hears Paul's case in Acts 25.
High priest in Jerusalem during the climactic Jerusalem episodes.
Associate in Troas with whom Paul left his cloak and books according to 2 Timothy.

Head of the Catechetical School of Alexandria; author of Protrepticus, Paedagogus, and Stromateis; synthesized Christian faith with Greek philosophy; key witness for Alexandrian baptismal theology, eucharistic practice, asceticism, and apostolic tradition.
Bishop of Rome associated with the First Epistle of Clement c. 95-96; key early witness to Roman church order and apostolic memory.
Roman centurion at Caesarea whose household is incorporated into the Jesus movement in Acts 10.
Pauline associate said in 2 Timothy to have gone to Galatia.
Synagogue ruler in Corinth who believes with his household in Acts 18.
Athenian woman named among those who believe Paul's message in Acts 17.
Pauline associate said in 2 Timothy to have gone to Thessalonica.
Silversmith in Ephesus who stirs opposition in Acts 19.
Athenian Areopagite named among those who believe Paul's message in Acts 17.
Wife of Felix who hears Paul in Caesarea in Acts 24.
Colossian coworker associated with the churches of Colossae, Laodicea, and Hierapolis.
Philippian envoy and coworker mentioned in Philippians.
Pauline associate said to remain at Corinth in 2 Timothy.
Philippian woman urged to live in harmony in Philippians.
Roman governor who hears Paul's case at Caesarea in Acts 23-24.
Roman governor who succeeds Felix and hears Paul's case at Caesarea in Acts 25.
Companion of Paul identified as from Derbe in Acts 20.
Roman proconsul of Achaia during Paul's Corinth hearing in Acts 18.
Jewish-Christian historian who traveled from Corinth to Rome under Bishop Anicetus; compiled succession lists of Roman bishops; five books of Memoirs (Ὑπομνήματα) preserved in fragments by Eusebius.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Author of the Shepherd of Hermas associated with the Roman church; brother of Pope Pius I according to the Muratorian Fragment.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Herodian ruler identified contextually as the Herod who persecutes the Jerusalem church in Acts 12.
Tetrarch of Galilee and Perea in the ministry narratives.
Bishop of Antioch martyred in Rome, early witness to episcopacy and eucharistic realism.

Bishop of Lyons (Lugdunum) and major anti-gnostic theologian; pupil of Polycarp; author of Against Heresies c. 180, the foundational work on apostolic succession, rule of faith, and refutation of Valentinianism and Marcionism.
One of the first called disciples.
Leading Jerusalem figure in the apostolic period.
Host in Thessalonica whose house is attacked during the mission in Acts 17.
Seed dataset focuses on public-ministry period claims.
Mission helper associated with Barnabas and Paul in Acts 13 and later tradition.
One of the first called disciples.
Prophetic forerunner active in the Judean wilderness.
Council member who requests the body of Jesus for burial.
Householder in Damascus on Straight Street with whom Saul is staying in Acts 9.
Disciple who leads those arresting Jesus in the passion narratives.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Roman saints.
Roman centurion who escorts Paul and shows him kindness at Sidon in Acts 27.
Believer greeted with Andronicus in Romans 16 and said to be in Christ before Paul.
Christian apologist and philosopher martyred in Rome, rich source for mid-2nd-century liturgy.
Brother of Mary and Martha in Bethany.
Prophet or teacher from Cyrene named in the church at Antioch in Acts 13.
Pauline companion traditionally associated with Luke and Acts.
Early believer associated with the church in Philippi.
Prophet or teacher at Antioch said to have been brought up with Herod the tetrarch in Acts 13.

Wealthy shipowner from Sinope in Pontus; came to Rome c. 140; rejected the OT and constructed a reduced canon; excommunicated from the Roman church c. 144; major polemical target of Justin, Irenaeus, and Tertullian.
Sister of Mary and Lazarus in Bethany.
Female disciple associated with Galilee, the passion, and the empty tomb narratives.
Mother of Jesus, present in the infancy, passion, and early Jerusalem-community traditions.
Believer in Rome commended for hard labor in Romans 16.
Member of the Twelve in Gospel and Acts lists.
Bishop of Sardis whose Peri Pascha is the earliest extant Christian homily, presenting Christ as Passover lamb and pre-existent Lord.
Early disciple from Cyprus who hosts Paul's company according to Acts 21.
Disciple from Cana associated with Philip in the Gospel of John.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 with his sister among the Roman saints.
Roman emperor associated with the Great Fire of Rome context and the subsequent persecution of Christians in Tacitus.
Jerusalem Pharisee who appears in the Gospel of John.
Believer in Laodicea associated with a house church in Colossians 4.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Roman saints.
Believer commended to Philemon and linked with Colossae in the Pauline correspondence.

Bishop of Hierapolis in Asia Minor; wrote the Exposition of Dominical Oracles (5 books) c. 95-110, preserved by Eusebius; heard John the elder directly; key early witness for oral apostolic tradition and millenarianism.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.

name_alt: Saul of Tarsus; Paul the Apostle
notes: Apostolic missionary central to Acts and several New Testament letters.
Beloved believer greeted in Romans 16 as one who labored much in the Lord.
Believer addressed by Paul in the personal letter concerning Onesimus.
Member of the Twelve; distinct from Philip the evangelist.
Missionary figure associated with Samaria and later Caesarea in Acts.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 among the Rome-network households.
Trusted woman commended by Paul in Romans 16.
Bishop of Smyrna martyred c. 155-156; disciple of the apostle John; correspondent of Ignatius and Rome.

Bishop of Ephesus; leader of the Asian Quartodeciman tradition; wrote to Victor of Rome c. 190 defending 14 Nisan observance by appeal to Philip the Apostle, John, Polycarp, and seven of his episcopal ancestors.
Roman prefect of Judea during the later ministry period.
Coworker of Paul associated with Corinth and Ephesus.
Leading Maltese host who receives Paul and his companions in Acts 28.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as chosen in the Lord.
Thessalonian companion of Paul named among the Acts 20 travel party.
Roman proconsul at Paphos who is said to believe in Acts 13.
Missionary coworker associated with Paul and the Jerusalem church.
Prophet or teacher named in the church at Antioch in Acts 13.
Disciple called during Galilean ministry.
Tanner in Joppa with whom Peter stays in Acts 9-10.
Companion of Paul identified as from Berea in Acts 20.
Figure beaten before Gallio's tribunal in Corinth in Acts 18.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as beloved by Paul.
Early Jerusalem believer remembered as a martyr in Acts.
Philippian woman urged to live in harmony in Philippians.
Disciple in Joppa known for good works and raised after death in Acts 9.
Student of Justin Martyr in Rome; wrote the Oration to the Greeks c. 155-165 and compiled the Diatessaron (harmony of four Gospels); later became an encratite ascetic and returned to the East.
Orator who presents the case against Paul before Felix in Caesarea in Acts 24.
Bishop of Antioch c. 169-183; wrote Ad Autolycum (three books) c. 180; first Christian writer to use the term Τριάς (Trinity); important witness for Logos theology.
Member of the Twelve and witness in the Johannine resurrection narratives.
Missionary coworker of Paul and co-sender of Pauline letters.
God-fearing Corinthian whose house next to the synagogue receives Paul in Acts 18.
Pauline coworker left in Crete and addressed in the Epistle to Titus.
Pauline associate left ill at Miletus according to 2 Timothy.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as one who labors in the Lord.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as one who labors in the Lord.
Pauline associate sent to Ephesus according to 2 Timothy.
Figure associated with the lecture hall in Ephesus where Paul reasons daily in Acts 19.
Believer greeted in Romans 16 as a fellow worker in Christ.
Bishop of Rome c. 189-199; central figure in the Quartodeciman controversy; threatened to excommunicate Asian churches over Easter dating; first bishop of Rome to write in Latin.
